narrative: As an Asian male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.50—above the typical 3.21 average—and a family income in the high bracket, you brought several strengths to your law school application. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your probability of failure at 45.5%, tipping the decision toward rejection to minimize the risk of poor bar outcomes. This prediction hinged most strongly on your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the average of about 5.5, which stood out as the top factor pushing the model toward forecasting a bar exam struggle. Counterbalancing that, your undergraduate GPA had the second-strongest influence, pulling the prediction in a positive direction by signaling stronger academic potential than most applicants. Your LSAT score of 32, lower than the average 36.7, played a lesser but still notable role in nudging the outlook slightly toward failure. Compared to typical students, your full-time enrollment status aligns with the vast majority at 92.7%, so it didn't sway things much either way. Overall, your lower Year 3 performance overshadowed your GPA advantage, placing you in a riskier category for first-time bar passage than the dataset's stronger profiles. While your high family income and above-average undergrad record are assets many applicants lack, the model prioritized proven later-stage grades as a key bar success indicator. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male student—many in similar demographics succeed with targeted improvements—but a data-driven call based on patterns in bar outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to or above average, and if possible, strengthen your recent academic decile through additional coursework or a post-bac program. Building a narrative around your GPA strengths in a reapplication, perhaps with bar prep endorsements, could also help. Admissions teams value persistence, so refining these areas positions you well for another shot at law school.
